metadata.title: Sławomir Liszniański portfolio data
metadata.description: Structured source data for a projection-based consultant portfolio.
notes: This file is intended to be the source of truth for a single-page portfolio app.
Projects are written once and re-used through projections, domains, capabilities, and tags.
Capabilities describe what a project proves; domains describe where the work happened.

person.name: Sławomir Liszniański
person.headline: Practical systems builder
person.shortBio: I design, build, debug, and improve complex systems across software, infrastructure, machines, buildings, field hardware, vehicles, and aviation.
person.positioning: I help organizations design and fix systems where correctness, reliability, maintainability, and real-world constraints matter.

hero.title: I build practical systems for people who still want to own their tools, infrastructure, and data.
body: I believe the Internet should make small teams, independent builders, and local organizations more capable — not more dependent on a few global platforms. AI and modern tooling make that vision practical again.

That is why my work crosses software, hardware, automation, aircraft, vehicles, energy, water, and infrastructure. Real independence is not just an app. It is the whole system: self-hosted software, durable infrastructure, embedded devices, automation, and physical systems that keep working when the cloud is not the whole answer.

tour.heading: How this works
tour.body: My work has crossed many different lanes. This site reflects that. Browse everything as one big picture, or choose a focused lens based on what brought you here.

projectionsSection.heading: Projections
projectionsSection.subhead: Choose the angle that matches what brought you here.

projectionsIndex.heading: Projections
projectionsIndex.body: Each projection is an audience-specific view of the same body of work — a different camera angle on one cross-disciplinary practice.

projectsIndex.heading: Projects
projectsIndex.body: A scannable index of every project. Filter by domain or capability if you want to narrow the view, or just skim the list.

projections.title: Cross-disciplinary systems builder
projections.headline: Software, infrastructure, machines, buildings, and field systems tied together by systems thinking.
projections.summary: A broad view for people who want the full picture: I work across software, electronics, aviation, vehicles, power, water, buildings, and operations.

projections.title: Software systems architect
projections.headline: Backend architecture, distributed workflows, financial correctness, databases, and frontend modernization.
projections.summary: For organizations needing reliable software systems, clear architecture, correctness-focused design, and pragmatic implementation strategy.

projections.title: Programming language designer
projections.headline: Syntax, type systems, packages, traits, compile-time rules, and runtime semantics.
projections.summary: For people interested in language/runtime design, compiler-facing decisions, package identity, generic systems, trait resolution, and semantics that stay understandable.

projections.title: Embedded and field systems builder
projections.headline: Sensors, BLE devices, diagnostics, automation, and hardware/software integration.
projections.summary: For systems that connect software to physical equipment, field conditions, sensors, diagnostics, and practical electronics.

projections.title: Physical systems builder
projections.headline: Aircraft, vehicles, fabrication, electrical systems, hydronics, workshops, and real-world infrastructure.
projections.summary: For projects involving mechanical systems, fabrication, diagnostics, construction constraints, energy, water, serviceability, and practical execution.

projections.title: Off-grid and infrastructure systems designer
projections.headline: Solar, wells, water, heating, power distribution, serviceability, and independent property systems.
projections.summary: For infrastructure projects where power, water, heat, buildings, maintenance, and long-term independence must work together.

capabilities.label: Systems integration
capabilities.description: Combining mechanical, electrical, software, operational, safety, and serviceability constraints into working systems.

capabilities.label: System architecture
capabilities.description: Designing software and infrastructure around correctness, durability, reliability, and maintainability.

capabilities.label: Distributed workflows
capabilities.description: Designing queue-backed, asynchronous, failure-tolerant workflows with clear request tracking and completion semantics.

capabilities.label: Financial correctness
capabilities.description: Designing transaction flows where balances, auditability, ordering, journal entries, and invariants matter.

capabilities.label: Database design
capabilities.description: Working with relational data, MariaDB, stored procedures, query boundaries, and business data correctness.

capabilities.label: Frontend architecture
capabilities.description: Designing maintainable single-page applications, component boundaries, state management, caching, and data-heavy UI.

capabilities.label: Asynchronous request processing
capabilities.description: Handling HTTP 202 workflows, request IDs, polling, status tracking, and dataset invalidation.

capabilities.label: Language semantics
capabilities.description: Designing clear rules for expressions, functions, lambdas, casts, copying, packages, and compiler behavior.

capabilities.label: Type system design
capabilities.description: Reasoning about type identity, traits, generics, receiver lookup, aliases, canonicalization, and safe composition.

capabilities.label: Package system design
capabilities.description: Designing package identity, versioning, dependency resolution, namespace trust, local caches, and repository discovery.

capabilities.label: Concurrency design
capabilities.description: Designing synchronization policies, fairness behavior, lock variants, batching, writer priority, and starvation tradeoffs.

capabilities.label: Regression-test thinking
capabilities.description: Turning design rules and past bugs into focused tests that prevent old mistakes from returning.

capabilities.label: Hardware/software integration
capabilities.description: Connecting software behavior with sensors, wiring, devices, controllers, diagnostics, and physical-world constraints.

capabilities.label: Sensor calibration
capabilities.description: Mapping raw sensor behavior to usable values, statistics, thresholds, and field-readable output.

capabilities.label: Field observability
capabilities.description: Making systems report useful status, history, faults, samples, and diagnostics in compact forms.

capabilities.label: Diagnostic thinking
capabilities.description: Finding root causes across systems where symptoms may come from multiple interacting layers.

capabilities.label: CAN bus integration and CAN bus debugging
capabilities.description: Troubleshooting CAN bus style systems involving wiring, termination, devices, protocol behavior, and configuration.

capabilities.label: Failure-mode analysis
capabilities.description: Asking what can fail, how it fails, what survives, and how the operator detects and recovers from it.

capabilities.label: Maintainability
capabilities.description: Designing systems so future inspection, service, replacement, and operation remain practical.

capabilities.label: Documentation discipline
capabilities.description: Maintaining structure, notes, design records, and decision history so complex work stays understandable.

capabilities.label: Long-horizon project execution
capabilities.description: Sustaining complex work over months or years without losing system context or decision continuity.

capabilities.label: Metal fabrication
capabilities.description: Cutting, fitting, drilling, fastening, welding-aware planning, alignment, inspection, and fabrication discipline.

capabilities.label: Safety-critical workmanship
capabilities.description: Working carefully where mistakes have real safety consequences and disciplined process matters.

capabilities.label: Mechanical systems
capabilities.description: Understanding machines, structures, linkages, routing, service access, forces, wear, and practical assembly.

capabilities.label: Electrical planning
capabilities.description: Planning circuits, panels, feeds, switching, controls, wire routing, and maintainable power distribution.

capabilities.label: Water system design
capabilities.description: Designing water supply, storage, pumping, pressure, monitoring, treatment, and serviceable layouts.

capabilities.label: Energy system design
capabilities.description: Planning solar, power distribution, backup, loads, mounting, and operational constraints.

capabilities.label: Infrastructure planning
capabilities.description: Designing property and facility systems around durability, access, cost, maintenance, and staged execution.

capabilities.label: Repair planning
capabilities.description: Assessing damage, sequencing repairs, estimating risk, identifying dependencies, and deciding what is worth fixing.

projects.title: S3-compatible financial workflow architecture
projects.summary: A durable asynchronous processing architecture for customer-scoped financial and business workflows.
projects.problem: Incoming requests needed to survive app downtime, retries, and spot-instance interruptions while preserving correct ledger ordering per customer.
projects.solution: Separated stateless request intake from durable S3-compatible queues, with ordered and concurrent work classes scoped by customer.

evidence.detail: Designed queue paths with pending, processing, done, and failed states for ordered and concurrent workloads.
Preserved per-customer serialization for ledger-affecting operations while allowing unrelated work to run concurrently.
Accounted for downtime, retry metadata, stalled processing, and spot-instance interruption behavior.
Kept the design compatible with S3 and MinIO so the system can be tested locally and remain cloud-agnostic.

software-systems-architect: Durable queue-backed backend architecture with clear request lifecycle.
Customer-scoped serialization for financial correctness without blocking unrelated customers.
cross-disciplinary-systems-builder: Shows failure-mode thinking across software, infrastructure, customer operations, and accounting correctness.

projects.title: GAAP-style ledger and payment flow design
projects.summary: Financial transaction design involving wallet prepayments, deferred revenue, receivables, hybrid payments, voids, tracked returns, and bad debt write-offs.
projects.problem: Payment and purchase workflows needed to produce correct journal behavior across cash, receivables, liabilities, clearing accounts, and customer wallets.
projects.solution: Modeled transaction flows around account-balance invariants, deferred revenue handling, receivable charging rules, and serialized ledger modifications.

evidence.detail: Designed flows where deferred revenue is consumed before receivables are charged, avoiding incorrect unconditional AR debits.
Designed Java-side orchestration that invokes stored procedures while preserving business-level transaction semantics.
Captured real-life scenarios such as wallet prepayments, hybrid payments, overpayments, voids, tracked returns, and bad debt write-offs.

software-systems-architect: Strong example of business rules becoming auditable backend behavior.
Shows exactness around accounting invariants rather than generic CRUD.

projects.title: Vue business application modernization
projects.summary: Modernization plan for a data-heavy business web application using Vue, custom UI abstractions, AG Grid, Pinia, Vue Router, TanStack Query, Tailwind CSS, and DaisyUI.
projects.problem: An older frontend needed a maintainable migration path without locking the product too tightly to a specific UI framework.
projects.solution: Designed a Vue architecture with project-local Node management, reusable UI primitives, data-grid support, persistent query cache, and cross-tab invalidation.

evidence.detail: Designed custom UI component abstractions so the underlying UI framework can be swapped with limited impact.
Planned frontend handling for HTTP 202 updates, request ID polling, dataset invalidation, and cross-tab refresh behavior.
Started with a simple client-side data model and reserved server-side complexity for actual performance needs.

software-systems-architect: Pragmatic modernization strategy for a real business application.
Balances data-heavy UI, caching, async backend updates, and long-term component flexibility.

projects.title: Drift — a systems programming language
projects.summary: A systems language that combines C++-style deterministic resource management with Rust-like ownership rules, in syntax that stays compact and readable. The project covers the language specification, compiler toolchain (Drift → DMIR → LLVM), standard library, and signed-module package system.
projects.problem: Systems programming usually forces a choice. High-level languages are pleasant but slow when you push the limits, often hiding cost behind a garbage collector. Low-level languages are fast but spend the programmer's attention on raw pointers, pointer arithmetic, manual ownership tracking, and concurrency models that don't compose. Teams end up swapping languages mid-stack, writing performance-critical code in something they don't fully trust, or fighting a borrow checker as much as the problem itself. Drift exists to refuse that binary: one language that holds up across the whole performance spectrum, with safety as the default and low-level control available only when you deliberately reach for it.
projects.solution: Design decisions that interlock rather than fight each other: explicit ownership and deterministic destruction (RAII) instead of GC; move-by-default with explicit copies and a Copy trait restricted to genuinely cheap types; traits and interfaces that compile down to what you would hand-write; virtual-thread concurrency with structured scopes so synchronous-looking code scales over a lightweight scheduler without async coloring; FFI through precise binary layouts and opaque ABI handles instead of pointer arithmetic; and cryptographically signed compiled modules so imports can be verified across machines. Escape hatches (lang.abi, lang.internals, @unsafe) exist but you have to ask for them by name. The compiler lowers Drift to DMIR (an SSA-based mid-level IR) and then to LLVM IR for code generation.
outcomes: Published language specification covering syntax, semantics, ownership, traits, generics, error handling, and concurrency.
Working Drift → DMIR → LLVM toolchain prototype with end-to-end test programs.
Standard library covering strings, collections, IO, error handling, virtual threads, and process spawning.
Package and module system with signed artifacts, deterministic offline builds, and project-local dependency caches.

evidence.detail: Explicit cast<T>(expr) instead of implicit coercion; keyword boolean operators (and/or/not); move-by-default with implicit moves only in consuming positions; Copy restricted to cheap types so duplication cost stays visible at the call site.
Cross-package trait identity, alias canonicalization, visibility rules, and deduplication so generic resolution stays predictable when types and traits flow across package boundaries.
Virtual threads with structured scopes (conc.scope, scope.spawn, .join) so synchronous-looking code scales over an epoll-based scheduler without forcing async coloring or callback rewrites.

Project-local dependency caches, single-version resolution per build, cryptographically signed compiled modules, and a canonical repository directory (drift-sources.json) — so builds stay reproducible and supply-chain trust is verifiable.
Design decisions land alongside the test cases that pin their behavior. Semantic drift between revisions is caught at CI rather than by the next user.

programming-language-designer: Refuses the high-level-vs-low-level binary: safety is the default, and low-level control is opt-in by name.
Whole-stack design from surface syntax through DMIR and LLVM codegen, with the package and trust model worked out at the same level of care.
software-systems-architect: Architecture-level decisions about versioning, dependency isolation, supply-chain trust, and reproducible builds — applied to a language ecosystem rather than a service stack.

projects.title: Drift standard library and POSIX runtime
projects.summary: The standard library that ships with Drift, plus the systems substrate underneath it: virtual-thread concurrency and structured scopes, lock-free synchronization primitives, core containers, and a POSIX runtime (x86_64 Linux scheduler, epoll reactor, fiber-based context switching, nonblocking IO).
projects.problem: A systems language without a strong standard library is a hollow promise. Every program ends up reinventing primitives that are easy to get subtly wrong: memory ordering for atomics, queue invariants for cross-thread work shipping, safe memory reclamation in lock-free code paths, scheduler fairness under load. Either the language never becomes practical for real systems, or the ecosystem fragments into competing third-party libraries that disagree on the basics. The stdlib has to make the right choices once, so application code doesn't have to.
projects.solution: Four interlocking surfaces. **std.concurrent** — Executor with configurable policies (min/max threads, queue limit, saturation policy), VirtualThread<T> with join/join_timeout/cancel, Future and FutureGroup for join-all/join-any, Scope for structured concurrency, Mutex<T>/MutexGuard<T> with RAII release, ARC for shared ownership. **std.sync** — atomic cells (AtomicBool/Int/Uint/Uint64) with explicit MemoryOrder (Relaxed, Acquire, Release, AcqRel, SeqCst), AtomicHandle<T>/AtomicRef<T> for opaque atomic pointers, an MpscQueue<T> for one-shot work shipping, and epoch-based reclamation primitives (EpochDomain, EpochParticipant, retire/advance) for safe memory reclamation in lock-free code. **std.containers** — Array, Deque, HashMap, HashSet, TreeMap, TreeSet, each with both move and borrow iterators, range types, and explicit invalidation events. **POSIX runtime (x86_64 Linux)** — fibers on carrier threads via custom drift_context.S assembly, epoll reactor for nonblocking IO on files / pipes / sockets, mmap-backed fiber stacks with guard pages so stack overflow faults instead of corrupts, pidfd-based process spawning.
outcomes: std.concurrent public surface frozen as the contract the runtime evolves against — VT lifecycle states, ConcurrencyError taxonomy, executor policy builder.
Working fiber-based scheduler on x86_64 Linux with cooperative cancellation and parking that does not block a carrier thread.
std.sync atomics, MPSC queue, and epoch-based reclamation usable from application code without dropping into @unsafe.

Container implementations with iterator traits formalized: SinglePass, MultiPass (independent cursors), Bidirectional, RandomAccessReadable/Permutable, plus IteratorInvalidated events that fire instead of silent corruption.

evidence.detail: std.concurrent — Executor with configurable policies, VirtualThread<T> with join/join_timeout/cancel and a documented lifecycle (NEW→READY→RUNNING→PARKED→FINISHED/CANCELLED), Future + FutureGroup for join-all/join-any, Scope for structured concurrency, Mutex<T> with RAII guards.

std.sync — atomic cells across Bool/Int/Uint/Uint64 with explicit MemoryOrder, AtomicHandle<T>/AtomicRef<T> for opaque atomic pointers, MpscQueue<T>, and epoch-based reclamation (EpochDomain, EpochParticipant, retire/advance) — the building blocks lock-free data structures actually need.

Iterator trait hierarchy (SinglePass, MultiPass with independent-cursor contract, Bidirectional, RandomAccessReadable/Permutable) drives both `for` lowering and the algorithms in std.algo; containers map onto it explicitly rather than ad-hoc.

Container operations on invalidated iterators raise IteratorInvalidated(container_id, op_id) instead of returning corrupt data; fiber stacks are mmap'd with guard pages so stack overflow faults instead of silently scribbling on the heap; epoll reactor parks VTs without blocking the carrier thread.

std.concurrent and std.sync are written down as a contract — frozen public surface, documented state machines, runtime-target boundaries (x86_64 Linux only, with a planned target-triple-based gate before adding ARM).

programming-language-designer: The standard library that ships with a language is the language — concurrency, lock-free primitives, containers, and the systems substrate are the part most users actually feel.
Iterator traits and container invalidation events are part of the language's contract with users, not an afterthought.
software-systems-architect: Scheduler design, lock-free queueing, epoch-based reclamation, and structured concurrency — the building blocks of any nontrivial concurrent system, designed once rather than reinvented per program.
Runtime-target boundaries treated as an explicit contract: host vs. target distinction, planned move to target-triple-based selection before adding architectures.

projects.title: ESP32 water level monitoring device
projects.summary: A MicroPython ESP32 field device for monitoring tank water level through calibrated resistance readings, BLE command access, time-series history, refill detection, and compact JSON reporting.
projects.problem: A field water system needed simple local observability without relying on a complex external service.
projects.solution: Designed sensor calibration, rolling windows, refill detection thresholds, persistent sample storage, partial-window reporting, and BLE CLI commands with partial matching.

evidence.detail: Calibrated resistance range around empty/full readings and exposed the sensor constants in reported metadata.
Designed compact JSON summaries for 24-hour, 3-day, 7-day, and 30-day windows with refill counts and interval statistics.
Combined ESP32, MicroPython, BLE access, local storage, and buck-converter power planning.
Added simulation/test-populate behavior that does not overwrite real persistent data.

embedded-field-systems-builder: Practical embedded field device with calibration, logging, BLE interaction, and compact operator-readable output.
Shows hardware/software integration around a real water-system need.
off-grid-infrastructure-designer: Connects water infrastructure with monitoring, historical visibility, and low-maintenance field behavior.

projects.title: Experimental aircraft build
projects.summary: Experimental aircraft build requiring structural assembly, metal fabrication, systems integration, wiring, avionics/CAN bus troubleshooting, upholstery/interior work, documentation, and safety-critical workmanship.
projects.problem: An aircraft build is a long-horizon systems project where structure, controls, fuel, electrical, avionics, interior, inspection, documentation, and workmanship all affect the final result.
projects.solution: Approach the build as an integrated system: controlled workmanship, careful documentation, staged assembly, inspection mindset, and debugging across mechanical and electrical layers.

evidence.detail: Integrates airframe, controls, fuel, electrical, avionics, interior, tooling, inspection, and documentation into one working aircraft.
Requires precise fitting, drilling, fastening, alignment, repeatability, and inspection discipline.
Involves avionics/CAN bus troubleshooting where wiring, termination, configuration, and device behavior interact.
Demonstrates the ability to sustain a complex multi-stage project without losing the relationship between decisions and final operation.
Aircraft work forces careful process because mistakes have direct safety implications.

physical-systems-builder: Metal fabrication, fitment, routing, assembly, wiring, inspection, and serviceability in a safety-critical physical system.
Shows practical execution across mechanical, electrical, interior, and documentation work.
embedded-field-systems-builder: Avionics/CAN bus debugging connects wiring, protocol behavior, device configuration, and physical installation details.
Shows troubleshooting across hardware, software-like configuration, and mechanical routing.
cross-disciplinary-systems-builder: A flagship example of one project acting as a soup of capabilities: fabrication, wiring, debugging, documentation, safety, and long-horizon execution.

media.title: RV-10
media.caption: Van's RV-10, a four-seat experimental aircraft built from a kit. The build touches structural assembly, electrical, avionics, fuel, controls, interior, and inspection across thousands of hours of work.

media.title: Lycoming IO-540 Thunderbolt
media.caption: Lycoming's IO-540 in Thunderbolt trim — a blueprinted, balanced, tighter-tolerance build of the standard 540-cubic-inch six. Firewall-forward layout has to keep cooling, fuel routing, and accessory placement all working together.
media.alt: Lycoming IO-540 Thunderbolt engine mounted on the RV-10

media.title: Garmin G3X Touch panel
media.caption: Touchscreen avionics stack — PFD, MFD, engine monitoring, and autopilot interface in one set of glass. Panel layout has to balance flight ergonomics with serviceability behind the firewall.
media.alt: RV-10 instrument panel with Garmin G3X Touch displays

media.title: Panel hookup
media.caption: Wiring up the avionics panel behind the glass. Connector pinouts, bus separation, fuse layout, and harness routing all have to be sorted before the panel ever shows a single screen.

media.title: LiFePO4 battery install
media.caption: Lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) battery on a custom-fabricated mount. Lighter than lead-acid for the same usable capacity, but the install has to account for hold-down loads, thermal management, and a BMS that plays well with the aircraft's charging system.
media.alt: Custom mount holding a LiFePO4 battery installed in the RV-10

projects.title: Off-grid property infrastructure
projects.summary: Planning and building independent property infrastructure involving solar, wells, water storage, pumps, heating, power distribution, buildings, and serviceability.
projects.problem: Independent property systems need power, water, heating, monitoring, and maintenance to work together rather than as disconnected purchases.
projects.solution: Treat the property as an integrated infrastructure system with attention to load planning, water pressure, storage, heating zones, access, future maintenance, and staged upgrades.

evidence.detail: Connects power, water, heating, monitoring, and building systems into a coherent property-level design.
Prioritizes access, isolation, monitoring, and the ability to modify systems later without destructive work.
Considers what happens when pumps, power, storage, or heat distribution fail and how the system can be inspected or recovered.

off-grid-infrastructure-designer: Direct example of independent infrastructure planning across power, water, heat, storage, and maintenance.
Shows a practical systems approach to property self-sufficiency.
physical-systems-builder: Connects electrical, hydronic, water, construction, and facility constraints into buildable systems.

projects.title: Hangar and workshop facility systems
projects.summary: Facility planning for a hangar/workshop involving slab use, floor coating, rooftop solar layout, radiant heating, electrical distribution, water, and long-term maintainability.
projects.problem: A hangar/workshop is not just a building shell; it must support aircraft, vehicles, tools, heat, power, coatings, water, solar, and future work habits.
projects.solution: Plan the facility as a maintainable technical workspace with attention to load paths, floor durability, hydronic zoning, solar mounting, electrical routing, and service access.

evidence.detail: Coordinated workshop use, aircraft storage, rooftop PV, slab/floor coating needs, heat, power, and long-term serviceability.
Evaluated solar panel layouts, orientation, roof pitch, wind/snow/load considerations, and mounting tradeoffs.
Favored designs that allow monitoring, later changes, access, and repair without burying avoidable complexity.

physical-systems-builder: Shows practical planning for a working technical facility, not just a finished building.
Combines structure, power, heating, surface durability, and workspace operations.
off-grid-infrastructure-designer: Relevant to solar, distributed power, heat, and serviceable building systems.

projects.title: Vehicle transformation and diagnostics
projects.summary: Automotive and diesel work involving diagnostics, repair planning, vehicle transformation, salvage evaluation, engine systems, CAN/JTAG/firmware research concepts, and practical mechanical execution.
projects.problem: Vehicle projects often involve mixed mechanical, electrical, sensor, software, structural, and cost-risk problems.
projects.solution: Use diagnostic thinking, staged repair planning, maximum-bid discipline, parts/risk assessment, and systems-level understanding before committing money or labor.

evidence.detail: Works across symptoms that may originate in mechanical damage, wiring, sensors, control logic, or configuration.
Evaluates repair difficulty, likely hidden damage, parts cost, structural risk, and whether a project is worth pursuing.
Researches ECU development paths, OEM reference logging, supervisor piggybacking, subsystem control, and injector driver architecture.

physical-systems-builder: Shows practical repair and transformation planning across mechanical, electrical, and structural layers.
embedded-field-systems-builder: Connects vehicle diagnostics with control systems, CAN-style communication, firmware concepts, and sensor behavior.
cross-disciplinary-systems-builder: Good example of software-like debugging applied to physical machines.

projects.title: Reader/writer lock policy design
projects.summary: Design exploration of reader/writer lock variants with different fairness, batching, writer-priority, and interleaving policies.
projects.problem: A single generic RW lock does not communicate the fairness and starvation tradeoffs required by different workloads.
projects.solution: Defined use-case-specific lock concepts such as Zipper-style interleaving and WriterPriority behavior so developers can choose based on policy, not just type name.

evidence.detail: Explored reader batching, writer interleaving, writer priority, starvation risk, and workload-specific lock semantics.
Focused on names and APIs that communicate policy clearly instead of hiding critical behavior behind a generic abstraction.

programming-language-designer: Shows attention to semantics, naming, and developer-facing clarity around concurrency primitives.
software-systems-architect: Shows willingness to design multiple explicit tools rather than force one abstraction across incompatible use cases.

goodFit: Financial ledgers (including blockchain) and payment systems
Practical planning for systems that combine software, hardware, machines, and infrastructure
poorFit: Pure staff-augmentation coding with no design authority
Highly bureaucratic environments that do not want assumptions challenged
Projects where correctness is less important than appearances
Work that requires pretending unrelated symptoms are unrelated when they are probably part of one system
engagementNotes: I am self-employed and not looking for a traditional job.
I am open to selective consulting where direct technical judgment and system-level thinking are needed.
I am most useful when the problem is messy, cross-disciplinary, and expensive to get wrong.

contact.preferred: Preferably by introduction, but direct inquiries will not be ignored.
